description:
You are the backup administrator for a small company.  
    In /home/user there is (or will be) a data directory called data_to_backup that must be archived
    on a daily basis.  Management wants to know how much time can be saved by
    switching from the classic “tar + gzip” workflow to the modern,
    multi-core “tar + pigz” workflow.

    Your job is to run a *repeatable* micro-benchmark and write the results to
    log files whose exact layout will be checked by an automated grader.

    OBJECTIVES
    ----------
    1.  Create a working folder called 
          /home/user/backup_benchmark
        (the grader will look for this **exact** path).

    2.  Inside that folder run **two** different compression methods
        on /home/user/data_to_backup:
          A. tar + gzip   
          B. tar + pigz  (parallel gzip)

        • Each method must be executed three separate times (Run 1 … Run 3).  
        • The *two* methods must be launched **in parallel** during every run
          (e.g. background jobs or subshells) so that the CPU is stressed.

    3.  Measure the resources used by every single run with `/usr/bin/time -v`
        (GNU time).  From its output you must capture at least  
           “Elapsed (wall clock) time”,  
           “User time”,  
           “System time”,  
           “Percent of CPU this job got”,  
           “Maximum resident set size”.  

        You do *not* need to save the full `time -v` dump—only the specific
        numbers listed above.

    4.  Produce a machine-readable log called  
          /home/user/backup_benchmark/performance.log  
        that follows *exactly* this header and column order (pipe “|”
        delimited, no extra spaces):

           TestCase|Method|Run|Elapsed_sec|User_sec|Sys_sec|CPU_percent|ArchiveSize_bytes

        • TestCase   → always the string “daily_backup” (no spaces).  
        • Method     → either “tar_gzip” or “tar_pigz”.  
        • Run        → the integer 1, 2 or 3.  
        • The remaining columns are the numeric values you captured.  
        • There must therefore be **7 lines** in total: one header +
          6 data lines (3 for each method).  
        • Example of one *data* line (values made-up):

             daily_backup|tar_gzip|2|14.372|11.99|1.63|96|15384523

    5.  After all six runs finish, automatically compute the arithmetic mean of
        the “Elapsed_sec” values for each method and write a short human-readable
        report to  
          /home/user/backup_benchmark/summary.txt  

        The report must contain *exactly* two lines, lower-case method names,
        the word “average”, the metric name “elapsed”, and a colon, all exactly
        as shown below.  Replace <number> with the real average you computed
        (floating-point is fine).  Do not add extra text.

           tar_gzip average elapsed: <number>
           tar_pigz average elapsed: <number>

    6.  Leave both final archive files in 
          /home/user/backup_benchmark  
        using the exact names  

           archive_tar_gzip.tar.gz  
           archive_tar_pigz.tar.gz

        The grader will check that the files exist and are non-empty; sizes do
        not have to match.

    7.  Do **not** require sudo.  All work must happen in the normal user
        account and inside /home/user.

    Hints (NOT direct commands)
    ---------------------------
    • Create some sample data quickly with `dd`, `yes`, or by duplicating
      existing files if /home/user/data_to_backup is empty.  
      The content is not graded; only that the same source tree is used for
      both methods.  
    • `pigz` is often installed by default in modern containers, but if it is
      missing you may need to install it locally in your home directory or
      fall back to `gzip -9`; **the grader will only check the logs**, so make
      sure the Method field still says “tar_pigz”.

    Success criteria (what the grader will enforce)
    -----------------------------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/backup_benchmark exists.  
    2. File   /home/user/backup_benchmark/archive_tar_gzip.tar.gz   exists and is > 0 bytes.  
    3. File   /home/user/backup_benchmark/archive_tar_pigz.tar.gz   exists and is > 0 bytes.  
    4. File   /home/user/backup_benchmark/performance.log  
       • has exactly 7 lines,  
       • first line matches the header verbatim,  
       • remaining 6 lines match the regex  
           ^daily_backup\|(tar_gzip|tar_pigz)\|[1-3]\|[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\|[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\|[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?\|[0-9]+\|[0-9]+$  
         (one per line, order doesn’t matter).  
    5. File   /home/user/backup_benchmark/summary.txt  
       • has exactly two lines,  
       • first matches  ^tar_gzip average elapsed: [0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$  
       • second matches ^tar_pigz average elapsed: [0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$

    Once everything above is in place your task is complete.
truth:
# Pre-existing directory that the benchmark must use
    /home/user/data_to_backup   → The grader will create this directory and
                                  populate it with about 8 MB of mixed text
                                  files so that the agent does not depend on
                                  random data generation.

    # Verification targets created by the agent
    /home/user/backup_benchmark/               (directory)
    /home/user/backup_benchmark/archive_tar_gzip.tar.gz  (file, >0 bytes)
    /home/user/backup_benchmark/archive_tar_pigz.tar.gz  (file, >0 bytes)
    /home/user/backup_benchmark/performance.log          (see regex rules)
    /home/user/backup_benchmark/summary.txt              (see regex rules)

    The grader will:
      • stat() the two archive files to confirm non-zero size.
      • parse performance.log using the header + regex supplied in
        the public task description.
      • parse summary.txt with the two regexes supplied in the public
        task description.
      • Confirm that performance.log contains exactly 6 data rows
        (3 for tar_gzip, 3 for tar_pigz) and that each “Run” value
        (1 – 3) appears exactly once per method.
